_**User**_

@instant-pot-chicken-adobo.md create a blog item on Arroz Caldo Using 8 quart Instant Pot. Use the recipe format here for this:

YIELD: 10-12 Servings
TOTAL TIME: ~45 minutes

INGREDIENTS

The Essentials:
10-11 cups (2.4-2.6 L) water or chicken broth
2.5 cups (500 g) glutinous rice (malagkit), rinsed well
1 kg (2.2 lbs) Chicken Drumsticks (bone-in)
6-7 inches (15-18 cm) fresh ginger, minced
10 cloves garlic, chopped
6-8 tablespoons (90-120 ml) fish sauce (patis)
2-3 Knorr chicken cubes or 1 tablespoon (15 g) powder
Black pepper to taste

Optional Additions:
Kasubha (safflower)
2-3 onions, diced

Toppings:
Kalamansi or lemon
Crispy garlic
Green onions
Hard-boiled eggs

INSTRUCTIONS

Step 1: Prepare
Mince your ginger and garlic. Rinse the glutinous rice thoroughly until water runs clear to prevent excessive gumminess.

Step 2: Create and Taste the Base (CRITICAL SAFETY STEP)
Before adding any meat, pour your water or broth into the Instant Pot. Add the ginger, garlic, onion (if using), chicken cubes, fish sauce, and black pepper. Stir well to dissolve the cubes.
TASTE THIS LIQUID NOW. It should taste slightly salty (saltier than a normal soup) because the rice will absorb the flavor. Adjust the fish sauce or salt now.
Do not proceed until you are happy with the liquid flavor.

Step 3: Add Chicken and Rice
Once you have finished tasting the liquid, add the rinsed rice and the raw chicken drumsticks. Stir well to ensure the rice is not clumped at the bottom.
From this point on, DO NOT TASTE the mixture again until it is fully cooked.

Step 4: Pressure Cook
Secure the lid, ensure the venting knob is in the Sealing position.
Select High Pressure for 25 minutes.

Step 5: Natural Release
Once the timer beeps, allow natural pressure release for 15 minutes. Do not use quick release, or the starchy liquid may spray out.
After 15 minutes, turn the venting knob to Release to let out any remaining pressure.

Step 6: Check Consistency
Open the lid and stir vigorously to break up the rice and release the starch.
If the mixture is too solid (which is likely with this ratio), add hot water 1 cup at a time until it reaches your desired soup consistency.
Taste one final time and add more fish sauce if the extra water diluted the flavor too much.

Explain why using instant pot is better using my old method using a rice cooker

---

<think><details><summary>Thought Process</summary>
I'll reuse the existing image `/images/arroz-caldo.jpg` for this post.

Drafting the new post as `instant-pot-arroz-caldo.md`. It mirrors the adobo template with frontmatter, an opening about the 8-quart batch, and a dedicated section comparing the Instant Pot to the rice cooker method. The recipe will include yield, ingredients, Instant Pot safety notes, and step-by-step instructions.

The comparison highlights four advantages of the Instant Pot over the rice cooker. It is faster, taking roughly 25 minutes versus 45–60 minutes for the congee cycle. It serves a larger batch, producing 10–12 servings instead of 4–6. Bone-in drumsticks release more collagen and flavor under pressure. The pressure cooking method also forces a separate taste test of the rice base before adding raw chicken, unlike the rice cooker where everything is dumped together.</details></think>

with open('data/voice-words.yaml') as f:

text = open('content/english/blog/instant-pot-arroz-caldo.md').read()
for w in data.get('words', []):

" 2>/dev/null

Created `content/english/blog/instant-pot-arroz-caldo.md` using the same structure as your adobo post.

**What's in it:**

- **Frontmatter** — slug `instant-pot-arroz-caldo`, same categories/tags pattern, reuses `/images/arroz-caldo.jpg`
- **Opening** — links to your [rice cooker version](/blog/set-it-forget-it-arroz-caldo/) and frames this as the big-batch 8-quart recipe
- **"Why Instant Pot instead of the rice cooker"** — compares both methods on:
  - Batch size (4-6 vs 10-12 servings)
  - Speed (~45 min vs ~1 hour)
  - Bone-in drumsticks under pressure vs thigh fillets in the rice cooker
  - **Taste-before-chicken workflow** (food safety — can't taste again until cooked)
  - Natural release (no quick release on starchy congee)
  - When each method still wins (rice cooker for Friday overnight; Instant Pot for crowds and faster turnaround)
- **Full recipe** — your ingredients, yield, and all 6 steps, including the critical taste-the-base step

The rice cooker post stays as the small Friday-night ritual; this one is the scaled-up pressure-cooker version. Want me to commit it or tweak anything (e.g. cross-link from the old post back to this one)?
